1. Introduction

WA Police Force personnel who create, edit or finalise a crime IR within the Incident Management System (IMS) perform the function of crime recording.

It is the responsibility of all personnel to record crime in accordance with this guidance material. It is the responsibility of supervisors and managers to review and monitor crime recording to ensure compliance.

Consistency and accuracy in the recording of crime incidents and associated offences is of critical importance to WA Police Force as it informs strategic, tactical and operational decision making.

It is also necessary to provide external stakeholders, such as the Government and community, with an accurate picture of the crime environment and to meet agency reporting obligations at a national level, by adhering to the National Crime Recording Standard 2008 (NCRS).

2. Aim

The aim of this document is to provide personnel with guidance on the creation, Quality Review Process and finalisation of crime Incident Reports. This guide provides instruction on the treatment of some key specific crime types and on the Outcomes to be applied to Incident Reports.

3. IMS Offence Outcome Status Codes (Outcome)

Upon the creation of an offence within an Incident Report, an Outcome of Under Investigation is automatically applied, which may be replaced with different Outcomes throughout the lifecycle of an Incident Report until the final Outcome (write-off) is applied. The correct selection of the final Outcome is of extreme importance as this will determine if the offence against which it is applied will ultimately be counted as a reported crime statistic at both a state and national level.

The following table provides a description of Outcomes and the impact on crime statistics:

Not Counted in Statistics To be used to finalise offences when:

Entered in Error an offence has been created in error

False Report a deliberate report of an offence that did not occur No Criminal Offence determined that an offence did not occur (includes mistaken reports and civil matters)

Offence Substituted an offence is being substituted with another offence Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 3 | P a g e

Counted in Statistics To be used to finalise offences when:

Offender Processed an offender is processed for that offence (includes all cautions, referrals and infringement notices)

Complaint Withdrawn an offence occurred, however, victim indicates they no longer support an investigation or charging of the

offender

Uncleared the offence occurred but remains un-resolved.

Applied when it has been determined no further lines

of inquiry exist, or a management decision has been

made not to investigate further

Insufficient Evidence evidence is not sufficient to proceed by way of charge against the offender for a recorded offence

Unable to Proceed / Charge legal impediment exists preventing further progress of an investigation or the charging of an offender; this

includes, Statute of Limitations, immature age, Public

Interest - DPP, death of victim/key witness/suspect

Under Investigation default Outcome when offence is not finalised Offences with an Outcome from the ‘Not Counted in Statistics’ section will not become a reported crime statistic, whilst offences having an Outcome from the ‘Counted’ section will be included in the reported crime statistics.

While an offence with an Outcome of Offence Substituted will not be counted, the offence which is substituted in its place will generally have an Outcome that is counted in the reported crime statistics. For example, if a Burglary with Intent is substituted for one of Burglary and Commit (when property theft is discovered) an Outcome of Offence Substituted will be applied to the Burglary with Intent offence and will not be counted. The offence of Burglary and Commit when finalised will instead be counted in the crime statistics.

Written justification for the selection of all Outcomes must be recorded in the Incident Report Running Sheet, with sufficient detail to enable quality review by a supervisor before finalisation. This is required regardless of which Outcome is applied.

Annexure A lists all Outcomes, and provides further explanation and definition.

4. Recording and Maintaining a Crime Incident / Offence

In alignment with NCRS, when creating, maintaining and quality reviewing a crime Incident Report, WA Police Force personnel need to, as far as practicable, ensure:

- Incidents are not the subject of more than one Incident Report.

- The matter being reported can be defined as a criminal offence under a written

law. Where it is clear that a complaint relates purely to a civil matter, accident or honest but mistaken belief (with no evidence or inference of criminality), an Incident Report should not be taken. However, where there is doubt, an Incident Report should be taken and the incident subject to investigation, with the appropriate final Outcome applied.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 4 | P a g e

- If more than a single Incident Report relating to a series of incidents that are

linked are created, that offences are not duplicated (the same offences against the same victims are not multiplied).

- Offences generated on an Incident Report relate specifically to that incident

(offences related to other matters for which a suspect is charged, that are not directly related to the incident, are not captured in the same Incident Report).

- There is no unnecessary creation of facilitation offences (see 4.2.1) and offences

that are inherently a component of the primary offence which defines an incident (e.g. creating offences of Assault Occasioning Bodily Harm and Common Assault in relation to the same incident).

- Offences are not multiplied beyond the circumstances of the incident (creating a

Burglary with Intent and a Burglary and Commit in relation to the same incident).

- Note that offences recorded in IMS do not need to match charges recorded in

the Prosecution system.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 5 | P a g e

4.1 Avoiding Incident Inflation (Multiple Reporting)

It is imperative that all criminal incidents are recorded once within IMS using the accepted practice of creating a uniquely numbered Incident Report.

Multiple Reporting occurs where more than one Incident Report is generated for the same incident, which results in multiplication of recorded offences. Some common reasons for this occurring include:

• Attending police not being aware an Incident Report has already been

commenced by the Police Assistance Centre (PAC), a Police Station or business area).

• A single incident being reported multiple times by different parties (e.g. the victim

/ a neighbour / security / multiple victims involved in the same incident).

• A victim contacting Police to add property or some other information to an

existing Incident Report, resulting in the generation of a new Incident Report due to police not being made aware of the previous report.

• A series of incidents occur involving multiple victims and resulting in creation of

a single Incident Report covering all offences, followed by creation of separate Incident Reports for individual victims.

To avoid the unnecessary creation of multiple Incident Reports relating to the same incident the following shall occur:

(cid:1) Where a CAD task is to be created for Police attendance, the Incident Report Number must be included in the CAD task which is to be reviewed by responding police prior to creating / submitting an Incident Report relating to the same incident.

(cid:1) Where appropriate, responding police are to contact the business area generating the CAD task (i.e. POC / SOC) to ascertain whether an Incident Report has been commenced and the Incident Report Number.

(cid:1) Inquiries are to be made with the person reporting to determine if the incident has been previously reported.

(cid:1) Provide the reporting person with an Incident Report Number. Ensure they are aware of the importance to declare that the matter has previously been reported (and quote the number) should further contact be required with police. This includes adding property items to an existing Incident Report.

(cid:1) A search is to be undertaken of the Incident Management System (IMS) to identify existing reports for the same incident (refer following).

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 6 | P a g e

Searching for Reports in IMS

Prior to creating an Incident Report, IMS should be interrogated against both the victim and the incident location to determine whether any recent reports have been submitted in relation to the same location or victim. If recent reports do exist, these should be reviewed to determine whether the incident has already been subject of an Incident Report.

Both the long form and the Rapid Data Entry (RDE) version of IMS support searching for previous Incident Reports against both the victim and incident location. The following process should be undertaken using IMS to expose any previously submitted Incident Reports related to the same incident:

Long Form version:

Location Search: From the first IMS Search screen, select ‘Advanced Search’, followed by ‘Location’. Enter and select the relevant location, which will open the ‘Maintain Location’ screen. From the links on the right of the screen, the ‘Incident’ link will be italicised (‘Incident’) this indicates there is a history recorded with respect to the location. Selecting that link will present a list of incidents, inclusive of all Incident Reports, with most recent at the top of the page. Each of those can be interrogated to determine if they are relevant to the matter currently being considered for Incident Report creation.

Victim Search: From the first IMS Search screen, enter victim’s name which will open the ‘Maintain Person’ screen. As with the Location Search, if the ‘Incident’ link is italicised, this indicates there is a history recorded with respect to the victim. Selecting that link will present a list of incidents, inclusive of all Incident Reports, with most recent at the top of the page. Each of those can be interrogated to determine if they are relevant to the matter currently being considered for Incident Report creation.

NOTE: (If there is more than one listing for the address or victim name, a screen with a list will appear and you will need to check each ‘Location’ or ‘Victim’ for an IR) Rapid Data Entry:

Location Search: If using RDE, after entering the location for the purposes of initiating an Incident Report, the blue ‘details’ arrow will be activated. Interrogating that will open a ‘Maintain Location’ screen. Selecting the ‘Incident’ link (if italicised) will present a history of the location, inclusive of previous Incident Reports. These can be interrogated to determine whether an incident is being reported more than once.

Victim Search: Entering the victim’s name will activate the blue ‘details’ arrow, which will lead to the ‘Maintain Person’ screen. Selecting the ‘Incident’ link (if italicised) will open a list of incidents recorded with respect to the victim, including previous Incident Reports. These can be interrogated to determine whether an incident is being reported more than once.

With regard to the RDE version, if it is detected that the incident under consideration for reporting has already been reported, the pending incident can be deleted without having to finalise a second Incident Report.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 7 | P a g e

4.2 Avoiding Offence Inflation (Multiplied Offences)

Offence inflation occurs when more offences are created or retained within an Incident Report than supported by the facts. The following are key causes of offence inflation that need to be avoided:

- Multiple reporting of the same incident (as per 4.1).

- Facilitation offences (see 4.2.1) that enable or are an element of the recorded

primary offence (e.g. damage to facilitate a burglary or theft from a motor vehicle).

- Failing to correctly finalise an initial offence with the appropriate Outcome when

generating an alternative offence.

- Creating additional offences on an Incident Report as a consequence of charging

multiple offenders, rather than linking offenders to the recorded offence (e.g.

creating additional offences of Burglary against a single burglary incident).

- Recording additional Burglary offences based on the number of occupants and

visitors, victims of theft, or number of persons having joint ownership of the premises/property (e.g. married couple).

- Recording both Burglary with Intent and Burglary and Commit offences on the

same Incident Report in relation to same premises and same victim.

- Creating multiple Burglary offences where both a residence and a shed on the

same property and owned by the same victim are entered.

- Creating a separate Incident Report for an offence of Steal Motor Vehicle and

Driving Dangerously / Recklessly following an Evade Police Incident, without properly substituting or finalising the initial offence of Steal Motor Vehicle that was recorded at the time the theft was reported, resulting in the creation of two Motor Vehicle Theft statistics (i.e. the initial Steal Motor Vehicle offence must have an outcome of Offence Substituted applied, with the new offence to be created within the existing Incident Report).

- Creating multiple theft offences where motor vehicle parts and property owned

by the same victim are stolen from a motor vehicle, or property is taken from different locations in a motor vehicle (e.g. handbag taken from the passenger cabin and tools from the cargo / boot area).

- Adding Stealing offences for each item of property stolen or creating Damage

offences based on number of items damaged.

- Creating additional offences based on what each offender is charged with, rather

than what is disclosed by, or defines, the incident.

The following provides guidance as to the correct application of offences to incidents.

4.2.1 Facilitation Offences

Facilitation Offences are those that enable, give rise to, or are an element of the primary offence by which the incident is defined. Examples include:

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 8 | P a g e

- Damage to enable the commission of a primary offence, such as smashing a

window to gain entry to a premises for the purpose of committing a Burglary offence or to access a motor vehicle to commit a stealing offence.

- Trespass onto a property to commit another offence (e.g. a Trespass to facilitate

a Burglary) should not be recorded.

- Common Assault where it forms part of a more serious Assault offence (e.g. do

not record a Common Assault and Assault Occasioning Bodily Harm for the same incident involving the same victim).

Recording Facilitation Offences unnecessarily inflates the recorded offence count. The principles of facilitation (outlined above) should be applied when recording all offences.

In addition, the following are mandatory requirements related to Damage and Trespass offences:

Damage: Where damage occurs facilitating another recorded offence, a Damage offence is NOT to be created. ONLY a description of the damage is to be recorded in the Property section of an Incident Report. Examples include:

- Burglary offence where the damage is caused to give access to a premises or to

break open locked internal doors or cabinetry to steal property.

- Stealing from a motor vehicle where damage is caused to give access to the

motor vehicle or locked receptacles to steal property.

- Stealing of a bicycle where damage involves cutting / breaking a bike lock.

- Cutting a fence to gain access to a yard from which property is stolen.

The rule does not inhibit substituting an Attempted Stealing offence or an Attempted Burglary with Intent for one of Damage where circumstances determine that an Attempted Stealing or Attempted Burglary is not supported by the available evidence.

Neither does it prevent charging an offender with a Facilitation Offence if supported by evidence, however the offence of Damage is not recorded as an offence in the Incident Report.

If a damage is reported that does not facilitate another recorded offence (as described above), it MUST be recorded as a Damage offence, as would occur with any other reported offence, which involves both creation of the offence and recording a description in the Property Panel of the Incident Report.

If during the commission of an offence additional unnecessary criminal damage is caused beyond the facilitation, such as the deliberate damage of property during a burglary or smashing more windows than necessary during theft from a motor vehicle, an offence of Damage should be created.

Trespass: An offence of Trespass should NOT be created in the following circumstances:-

- Trespass onto a property to commit another offence (e.g. a Trespass to facilitate

a Burglary) should not be recorded.

This rule does not prevent substituting a Burglary offence for a Trespass if it is determined only a Trespass has occurred.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 9 | P a g e

4.2.2 Recording and Finalising Offences

The following provides guidance on recording and finalising the most common offence types (scenarios to assist understanding are included in Annexure D):

Burglary Offences

1. With regard to the NCRS, a Burglary offence is identified as a crime against the

premises and not a person or business (albeit the person or business would be recorded as the victim) and is to be recorded having regard to the following:

- A Burglary can only be recorded with respect to structures capable of being

secured at the time of the offence. A veranda, carport or other structures that are not fully enclosed could not be considered capable of being secured at the time of the offence, therefore a Burglary cannot have occurred. If an offender entered such an area and stole property, offences of Trespass or Stealing could be considered.

Notwithstanding this, should a structure temporarily not be secured, for example a residence that has suffered damage, being renovated or some other temporary reason, a Burglary offence should be recorded. This does not however apply to structures that are under construction and have not reached ‘lock-up’ stage.

Whilst being transported, a sea container would not be considered a place as defined in section 400 of the Criminal Code. However, once placed onsite it could then become a place (structure) as defined, especially if the intention of its use is for storage. As such an offence of Burglary should be recorded if the sea container was broken in to.

- Where a burglary occurs on an individually occupied premises (e.g. residential

home), only one Burglary offence must be recorded irrespective of the number of joint owners, occupants, visitors, persons having property stolen or damaged or persons charged. Further, only one Stealing offence should be created, irrespective of the number of victims; with each victim linked to the single Stealing offence.

- Where multiple structures at the same location have been burgled, a single

Burglary offence is to be created where the individual structures are occupied by the same victim (i.e. house, separate garage and shed on a residential block or multiple buildings on a commercial block). The exception is where the same victim owns a business premises and an attached residence which are burgled, in which case separate offences must be created (the business and residential premises must be distinctly separate and not just operating a business from home).

- Where a Burglary has been committed on multiple structures at the same

location but occupied by different tenants, separate Burglary offences must be created (e.g. industrial workshops with different tenants). This includes separate storage areas in an apartment complex.

- Where a Burglary is committed on individual areas in a building that are leased,

rented or occupied separately and each area is capable of being secured from the others, a Burglary offence needs to be created for each (e.g. shops within Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 10 | P a g e

a shopping centre; occupied hotel rooms; individual apartments in an apartment building etc.).

- Where multiple hotel / motel rooms are broken into, only one Burglary offence

is to be created where rooms are unoccupied, with the victim recorded as the hotel / motel. Any that are occupied must be subject of a separate Burglary offence, with the victim recorded as the occupant.

- Where a Burglary occurs on a number of structures under development at a

defined single location (i.e. units under construction), that are at ‘lockup stage’, only a single Burglary offence is created where the builder is considered to be the sole victim (all structures owned by the builder). If any of the individual structures are owned by a different victim, a separate Burglary offence is to be created.

2. An Attempted Burglary, Burglary with Intent and Burglary and Commit are all

counted as a Burglary offence within crime statistical reporting and consequently only one of these should be recorded at any one time in a crime Incident Report in relation to a single victim, incident and premises.

3. If a victim wants to report more than a single Burglary on the same premises (a

premises having been burgled at different times), each offence must be subject of its own Incident Report and Incident Reports should be linked.

4. Where multiple offenders are charged with a Burglary offence involving a single

incident they are to be linked to the single Burglary offence on the Incident Report without creating additional unnecessary Burglary offences. It is immaterial that multiple offenders are charged with different types of Burglary offences (e.g.

Offender A charged with Burglary and Commit, Offender B charged with Burglary with Intent and Offender C charged with Attempted Burglary with Intent).

5. Where an investigation finds that a Burglary offence HAS occurred, however the

available evidence is not sufficient to enable the charging of an offender with the Burglary offence, an Outcome of Insufficient Evidence is to be applied to the Burglary. This will ensure the Burglary offence is counted as a crime statistic.

6. Where an investigation finds that a Burglary offence HAS NOT been committed

(i.e. mistaken belief) an Outcome of No Criminal Offence needs to be applied to that offence, which will ensure the offence is not counted as a crime statistic.

7. Where a Burglary with Intent is initially recorded and stolen property is later

reported, an Outcome of Offence Substituted must be applied to the initial offence and a Burglary and Commit and Stealing (General) offences created.

Stealing of Motor Vehicle

For the purpose of recording the offence, Stealing of Motor Vehicle within IMS, a motor vehicle:

- Is built to be propelled by a motor that forms part of the vehicle.

- Is self-propelled that is not operated on rails and includes a trailer, semi-

trailer or caravan while attached to a motor vehicle,

- Does not include a power assisted pedal cycle.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 11 | P a g e

- Must be eligible for registration for use on public roads.

The above combines the definition of a motor vehicle pursuant to the Road Traffic (Administration) Act 2008 and the National Crime Recording Standards 2008.

1. If a caravan, trailer or other towed vehicle is attached to the motor vehicle when

stolen only one Stealing of Motor Vehicle offence should be created. Both are to be recorded in the Incident Report ‘Vehicle’ Panel separately. If the motor vehicle is recovered, however the caravan, trailer or other towed vehicle is not, an offence of Stealing (General) must be added.

2. While caravans, trailers and other towed vehicles may be registered for use on

roads, they are not self-propelled and consequently if stolen should result in creation of a Stealing (General) offence and NOT a Stealing of Motor Vehicle offence.

3. Stealing of motor vehicles that are not eligible for registration should be recorded

in IMS as an offence of Stealing (General) and NOT a Stealing of Motor Vehicle offence. This does not inhibit a charge of Stealing of Motor Vehicle where deemed appropriate. Examples include:

- Go-carts

- Golf Cart

- Gopher

- Motorised wheelchairs

- Dirt bikes ineligible for registration

- Power Assisted Pedal Cycle

4. IMS allows a user to select three different motor vehicle theft offence descriptions:-

- Stealing of Motor Vehicle

- Stealing of MV and Drive Dangerously

- Stealing of MV and Drive Recklessly

As each one of these offences will be counted as the theft of the same motor vehicle, only one offence can be ‘live’ with reference to the same motor vehicle at any point in time. If upgrading the offence from Stealing of Motor Vehicle to either of the other two, the Outcome of Offence Substituted MUST be applied to the initial offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle. A new Incident Report is NOT to be created.

5. An offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle must be created for every motor vehicle

stolen, irrespective of whether the motor vehicles are owned by the same victim.

6. Where a stolen motor vehicle is recovered and it is discovered that the registration

plate(s) or other property that was in the vehicle at the time it was stolen, are missing, a new offence of Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) must be added for the outstanding registration plate(s) or other property.

7. A separate offence is NOT to be created for the stealing of vehicle keys if the keys

were in the motor vehicle at the time of its theft. Neither should a Stealing of Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) offence be created for any other property in the motor vehicle at the time of theft. These items are to be recorded by description ONLY in the Property screen of the Incident Report. If the motor vehicle is recovered and Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 12 | P a g e

the property or keys are still missing, an offence of Stealing (General) must be added.

Overdue Rental Motor Vehicles (Hire Cars)

1. The procedure by which the WA Police Force will accept reports of Overdue

Rental Motor Vehicles is attached as Annexure B. Also refer to CQ – 01.02 Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle Policy.

Stealing From Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts)

1. An offence description of Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) is

available for selection from within IMS, which enables the capture of data related to this crime type. For prosecution purposes, the correct offence is one of Stealing, Section 378 Criminal Code.

2. This offence description should only be applied to Motor Vehicles as defined under

the heading of Stealing of Motor Vehicle . Where contents or parts of vehicles (e.g.

Trailers and caravans) other than those defined as ‘Motor Vehicles’ are stolen, the correct offence to apply is Stealing (General).

3. When an Attempted Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) offence is

initially recorded and later upgraded to Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts), the Attempt offence MUST be removed by applying an Outcome of Offence Substituted.

4. Where the victim for the stealing of component parts from a motor vehicle (e.g.

sound system; wheels etc.), is the same for the stealing of non-vehicle related property (e.g. wallet, GPS etc.), ONLY ONE offence of Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) is to be created. Where property belonging to other persons is also stolen, they should be entered as victim against the same offence and no additional offences should be added.

5. Where stealing from a motor vehicle includes removal of property from different

areas of the vehicle (i.e. property taken from the passenger area and the cargo / boot area of a motor vehicle), ONLY ONE Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) is to be created.

6. The Stealing of registration plate(s) should be recorded as an offence of Stealing

from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) if the registration plates have been stolen from a motor vehicle. If the Registration plates have been taken from other than a motor vehicle, such as from a trailer or a garden shed, a Stealing (General) offence is to be created.

7. If both Registration Plates are stolen from a motor vehicle, ONLY ONE offence of

Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) is to be created.

Stealing from Retail Premises (Shoplift)

1. The IMS offence description of Stealing from Retail Premises (Shoplift) has been

created to enable the capture of data related to this crime type. For prosecution purposes, the correct offence to be applied is one of Stealing, Section 378 Criminal Code.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 13 | P a g e

2. Irrespective of the number of items stolen or offenders charged in respect to a

single shoplifting incident involving a single victim, ONLY ONE Stealing from Retail Premises (Shoplift) offence shall be recorded (e.g. two offenders working together in stealing multiple items from a shop will be recorded as a single offence).

3. If a shop is broken into and property is stolen an offence of Burglary and Commit

and Stealing (General) should be added, NOT Burglary and Commit and Stealing from Retail Premises (Shoplift) offence as it occurred outside of business hours.

Fuel Drive-offs: Stealing (General)

1. The procedure by which the WA Police Force will accept reports of Fuel Drive-offs

(FDO) is attached as Annexure C. Also refer to CQ – 01.01 Fuel Drive-off Policy.

Stealing (General) Offences

1. The IMS offence description previously known as Stealing is now Stealing

(General). For prosecution purposes, the correct offence to be applied is one of Stealing, Section 378 Criminal Code.

2. Attempted Stealing (General) and Stealing (General) are both counted as

separate offences within statistical reporting and consequently only one should exist on an Incident Report at any one time with respect to the same victim, property and individual incident.

3. Where an Attempted Stealing (General) is initially recorded and property is later

reported as having been stolen, a Stealing (General) offence is to be created and an Outcome of Offence Substituted applied to the initially recorded Attempted Stealing (General) offence.

4. Irrespective of the number of items stolen or offenders charged in respect to a

single incident involving a single victim, ONLY ONE Stealing offence shall be recorded.

5. Whenever a Stealing (General) offence is recorded, a description MUST be

included in the Incident Report ‘Property Panel’.

Fraud Offences

1. Two offence descriptions are selectable from within IMS:

- Fraud – Credit / Debit Card Offence;

- Fraud – Non-Credit / Debit Card Offence.

2. The offence description of Fraud – Credit / Debit Card Offence is to be selected

when an incident involves the use of stolen credit / debit cards to obtain property.

3. For every transaction involving the use of a stolen credit / debit card, an offence

of Fraud – Credit / Debit Card Offence must be created on the Incident Report.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 14 | P a g e

Assault Offences

1. On creation of an Incident Report, record only ONE Assault related offence (being

the most serious) for each victim for an individual incident, irrespective of the number of offenders or their potential individual culpability.

2. If an Assault offence is to be downgraded or upgraded, on recording of the new

offence the initial offence is to have the Outcome of ‘Offence Substituted’ applied.

3. Where multiple offenders are charged with the same offence against the same

victim, they are to be linked to the single Assault offence. DO NOT create an additional offence of the same type for each offender.

Family and Domestic Violence

1. The policy previously issued has changed relating to the application of the

Outcome, ‘Insufficient Evidence’ and ‘Offender Processed’. Previously these two Outcomes were the only two that could be applied to offences that related to matters of Family and Domestic Violence. Now, the most appropriate Outcome is to be applied as is the case with all other crime types. However the use of all other Outcomes MUST be thoroughly justified in all cases and may be subject to further scrutiny.

(Refer Annexure A for list of outcomes).

Property Damage

Where any damage is caused that is NOT caused for the sole purpose of facilitating a Stealing or Burglary related offence (including attempts), a Damage offence MUST be recorded on the Incident Report and a description of the damage entered into the ‘Property Panel’.

1. Damage and Burglary: In circumstances where damage is caused for the sole

purpose of facilitating entry to a premises for the purpose of burglary, or to access locked internal doors or cabinetry, and a Burglary offence has been created for the incident, an offence of Damage is not to be recorded on the Incident Report;

however, a description of the damage MUST be recorded in the ‘Property Panel’ of the Incident Report.

If during the burglary damage is caused beyond what is necessary to facilitate the burglary (e.g. property items, furnishings, walls, doors etc. are deliberately damaged or destroyed), a Damage offence MUST be recorded, with a description also recorded in the ‘Property Panel’.

2. Damage and Stealing from / of Vehicles: If damage is caused to a vehicle or

motor vehicle for the sole purpose of facilitating the stealing of property (contents or parts), or the vehicle or motor vehicle itself, and a Stealing or Attempted Stealing related offence has been recorded for the incident (i.e. Stealing of Motor Vehicle, Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts), Stealing (General) or Attempts to commit these offences), a Damage offence is NOT to be recorded; however, a description of the damage MUST be recorded in the ‘Property Panel’ of the Incident Report.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 15 | P a g e

Where deliberate damage is caused to a vehicle or motor vehicle that is NOT caused for the sole purpose of theft (e.g. smashing more windows than necessary or deliberately damaging or destroying contents or parts of the vehicle or motor vehicle), a Damage offence MUST be recorded and description entered in the ‘Property Panel’.

3. Damage and Stealing: If damage is caused to any property for the sole purpose

of facilitating theft and a Stealing or Attempted Stealing related offence has been created for the same incident, an offence of Damage is NOT to be recorded;

however a description MUST be entered into the ‘Property Panel’ of the Incident Report.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 16 | P a g e

5. Quality Review Process

All personnel who create, edit or finalise a crime Incident Report must follow the Quality Review Process. These processes assist to reduce incorrect data being recorded or retained.

Incident Report creation: Personnel involved in the creation of an Incident Report must ensure that:

- An Incident Report (and associated offences) is only created when there are

indications a criminal offence (as defined by written law), may have been committed. If there is no evidence or inference of criminality, the matter must be resolved other than by police investigation and a criminal Incident Report should not be created. If any doubt exists, an Incident Report should be generated.

- Incidents are not subject of more than one Incident Report.

- No more offences than necessary and appropriate are included on an Incident

Report (create offences that define the incident and avoid unnecessary duplication or including offences that facilitate the primary offence).

- Offences are correctly linked to victims.

- Practice rules relating to managing Fuel Drive-off and Overdue Rental Motor

Vehicles incidents are complied with.

- Offences of Stealing of Motor Vehicle have only been applied to motor vehicles,

as defined within the Crime Recording User Guide.

- An offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle is created for every stolen motor

vehicle; regardless of whether they are owned by the same victim.

Incident assessment, investigation and resolution: Personnel who assess, investigate, and, or finalise an investigation must ensure:

- No other Incident Reports are in existence relating to the same incident, which

may result in offence multiplication.

- Only necessary and appropriate offences are included on an Incident Report

and offenders and victims are linked to offences to avoid offence multiplication (i.e. multiple Burglary offences where a single offence occurred).

- Every offence in an Incident Report has the most appropriate Outcome applied,

with the reason for decision contained within the Running Sheet of the Incident Report. Offences that do not directly relate to the recorded incident have not been included in the Incident Report (e.g. If an offender charged with a Burglary is also charged with possessing drugs / weapons that have no relationship with the Burglary incident, therefore the drugs and weapons offences should be recorded on a separate Incident Report).

- Offences of Stealing and Damage have not been created based on the number

of items stolen or damaged.

- Facilitation Offences have not been included in the Incident Report (specifically,

damage in relation to gaining access to a premises or a motor vehicle for the purpose of burglary or theft, have not been included or retained on an Incident Report).

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 17 | P a g e

- Correct Outcomes have been applied and reason for selection has been

recorded within the Incident Report.

- Offences of Stealing of Motor Vehicle have not been created where the vehicle

does not meet the required definition.

- An offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle has been created for every stolen motor

vehicle; regardless of whether they are owned by the same victim.

- There is no more than one ‘live’ offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle, Stealing

of Motor Vehicle and Drive Dangerously, or Stealing of Motor Vehicle and Drive Recklessly related to the same motor vehicle.

Supervisor Review: The Supervisor review is considered the most important component of the Quality Review Process to ensure the accuracy and completeness of Incident Reports and subsequently reported crime statistics.

Irrespective of whether an Incident Report is filed based on a preliminary assessment only or following an investigation, a supervisor quality assurance review must be undertaken of every Incident Report before filing to ensure as far as practicable that:

- Only incidents that have been clearly determined to be criminal remain subject

to an Incident Report.

- Every offence in an Incident Report has the most appropriate Outcome

applied, with the rationale recorded in the Running Sheet of the Incident Report.

- Only necessary and appropriate offences are included in an Incident Report

and that offenders and victims are linked to offences to avoid offence multiplication.

- Offences that do not directly relate to the recorded incident are not included

in the Incident Report.

- Offences of stealing and damage have not been created based on the number

of items stolen or damaged.

- Facilitation Offences have not been included in the Incident Report.

- Offences of Stealing of Motor Vehicle have not been created where the vehicle

does not meet the required definition.

- An offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle has been created for every stolen

motor vehicle; regardless of whether they are owned by the same victim.

- There is no more than one ‘live’ offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle, Stealing

of Motor Vehicle and Drive Dangerously, or Stealing of Motor Vehicle and Drive Recklessly related to the same motor vehicle.

(IR) in IMS once finalised.

As part of this process, it is an ongoing expectation that Sergeants and above review the quality of an IR prior to finalisation. To ensure a high quality is consistently achieved, all Sergeants and above are required to understand and apply the Quality Review Process (Annexure F) and the Direct File Checklist. Annexure B: Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle Practice Guide This practice only relates to rental motor vehicles that have not been returned by a hirer in accordance with the rental contract. This practice does not apply to rental motor vehicles that are stolen from the rental premises or a hirer.

Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle Practice

1. Complaint received by telephone at the Police Assistance Centre (PAC) or

local police station. Call taker to obtain from the complainant a 24-hour contact phone number.

2. Call taker to add a General / High Priority IMS alert against the Vehicle of

Interest (VOI) with the narrative text advising:

Vehicle of Interest reported as an overdue rental motor vehicle [date, time, rental company, after hours contact number]. No IR taken. Contact rental company for further advice / action.

Alert is to be set with no expiry date and to be removed upon Police being notified the motor vehicle has been returned or located.

3. Complainant to be advised to complete an Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle

Reporting Pack and submit to the local police station.

4. Local police to review submitted Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle Reporting

Pack. An Incident Report with a Stealing of Motor Vehicle offence to be created (and the

Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle IMS alert removed) if any of the following criminality indicators exist:

(cid:1) The hirer has signed a rental contract including personal details, such as address, name and telephone number, which are suspected, or confirmed, as being false.

(cid:1) On being contacted by the rental company, the hirer expressly refuses to return the motor vehicle and has been advised by the rental company that consent to continue to use the motor vehicle has been withdrawn.

(cid:1) The motor vehicle has become a ‘Vehicle of Interest’ for another matter.

(cid:1) Any other factor that could give rise to suspicion of criminality.

If no indications of criminality exist, an investigation need not be conducted, and no further action is required at this time.

5. An investigation can commence.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 21 | P a g e

On the occasion the VOI is located by Police before the Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle Reporting Pack is received, Police are to immediately contact the rental company on the 24-hour phone number provided. The rental company is to arrange recovery of the vehicle and Police to remove the IMS alert.

If Police are unable to make contact with the rental company, driver and occupant details to be obtained, and the driver advised to return the VOI. The driver and occupant details can be provided to the rental company.

Overdue Rental Motor Vehicle Reporting Pack can be found on the Crime Recording and Reporting Intranet Page and the WA Police Website.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 22 | P a g e

Annexure C: Fuel Drive-off Practice Guide

From 1 June 2018, the Western Australia Police Force will no longer accept the Service Station Fuel Non-Payment Report form or any type of fax reports as a method for reporting fuel drive-offs.

1. Complaint received by telephone at the Police Assistance Centre (PAC) or local

police station.

2. Call taker to search IMS against registration number and registered owner. A

Priority 1 - 7 CAD job and/or BOLO to be created if any of the following alerts exist:

(cid:1) Stolen Motor Vehicle

(cid:1) Vehicle or person wanted for questioning

(cid:1) An Arrest Warrant

(cid:1) Person is an arrestable suspect (dependant on nature and circumstance) (cid:1) Vehicle is a VOI (dependant on nature and circumstance)

3. Call taker to add a General / Low Priority IMS alert against the vehicle with the

narrative text advising “Subject of Fuel Drive-off incident [date, time, location and amount and value of the fuel]”. Alert set to expire and automatically be removed, six months from submission date.

4. Call taker to assess if criminality indicators exist. The service station will be

advised to complete a Fuel Drive-off Reporting Pack and submit to the local police station, if any of the following exist:

(cid:1) VOI is subject to a current FDO IMS Alert.

(cid:1) VOI is a Stolen Motor Vehicle or Registration Plate(s) are reported stolen.

(cid:1) Registration plates are missing, are false or altered to avoid detection.

(cid:1) There is an overt act / behaviour suggestive of an attempt to avoid payment or disguise identity, such as:

(cid:2) VOI departs at high speed

(cid:2) Attempt to disguise face

(cid:2) Attempt to disguise registration plate (i.e. standing in front of plates)

(cid:2) Attempt to confuse service station console operator (i.e. multiple fuel pump applications).

If no indications of criminality exist, an investigation need not be conducted, and no further action is required.

5. Local police to review submitted Fuel Drive-off Reporting Pack and create an

Incident Report with a Stealing (General) offence if criminality indicators exist. An investigation should then commence.

Annexure D: Offence Recording Scenario Guide

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 23 | P a g e

Burglary: For burglary the victim is considered to be the premises.

Scenario 1: An offender smashes a rear window of a house to gain entry. Once inside property is stolen.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1.

• Stealing (General) x 1.

No damage offence is to be created as the damage was caused only to facilitate the burglary. The damaged property must still be recorded in the Property Panel of the IR.

Scenario 2: An offender smashes a window at the rear of a house to gain entry. Once inside, the offender damages a wall mounted television by throwing a heavy ornament at the screen, before stealing other property.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1.

• Stealing (General) x 1

• Criminal Damage x 1 Sect: 444 Criminal Code. (Damage to the television was

wilful, not facilitation or accidental).

Scenario 3: An offender smashes a window and enters a house. Once inside, the offender breaks open a locked cabinet to steal property, damaging the cabinet. The offender leaves the house with stolen property.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1.

• Stealing (General) x 1.

No damage offence to be created as damage to gain entry to the premises and cabinet was only to facilitate the burglary offence. The damaged property must still be recorded in the property section of the IR.

Scenario 4: An offender smashes a window to enter a house owned by a number of persons (i.e. married couple or multiple persons with joint ownership) and steals property belonging to multiple occupants.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1 (the number of premises owners is immaterial. As the

premises is considered the victim in burglary offences, a single offence only is created).

• Stealing (General) x 1 (irrespective of number of victims of property theft, a single

Stealing offence is created with all victims being listed to the offence).

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 24 | P a g e

Scenario 5: An offender breaks into both the main house and a garden shed on a single residential location and steals property from both.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1. (Same victim on same property).

• Stealing (General) x 1.

Scenario 6: Five visitors are staying with friends who occupy a rented residence. A burglary occurs during which property belonging to the renting occupants and all visitors is stolen.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1 (as this is a rental premises, the victim will be recorded

as the renter, not the premises owner).

• Stealing (General) x 1 (irrespective of number of victims of property theft, a single

stealing offence is created with all victims being listed to the offence).

Scenario 7: An offender breaks into a storage facility and once inside breaks into five individual storage areas leased by different persons and steals property from each.

Offences to record:

• Burglary with Intent x 1. (1 x burglary on main structure with own victim).

• Burglary and Commit x 5. (5 x burglary on the individually rented storage areas).

• Stealing (General) x 5. (5 x stealing from the individual storage areas).

Scenario 8: An offender forces a street access door into the foyer of a secure apartment building, following which the offender breaks into four apartments and steals property from each.

Offences to record:

• Burglary with Intent x 1 (victim is building owner or body corporate).

• Burglary and Commit x 4 (victims are the owners / occupants of the individual

apartments).

• Stealing (General) x 4 (one for each Burglary and Commit).

Scenario 9: An offender breaks into an occupied hotel room and steals property belonging to the guest.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1. (the guest is recorded as the victim).

• Stealing (General) x 1. (in the name of the guest).

Scenario 10: An offender breaks into an occupied hotel suite and steals property belonging to both the hotel and the guest.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1 (the guest is recorded as the victim).

• Stealing (General) x 1 (victims are recorded as both the hotel and the guest,

linked to the single stealing).

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 25 | P a g e

Scenario 11: An offender breaks into 4 unoccupied hotel suites and steals property belonging to the hotel from each suite.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1 (in name of the Hotel).

• Stealing (General) x 1 (in name of the Hotel).

Scenario 12: An offender climbs a fence into the rear yard of a house and enters the carport (not securable at the rear) and steals property. At the time the roller door is in the closed and locked position.

Offences to record:

• Stealing (General) x 1 (At the time of the offence the carport is not capable of

being secured and consequently a burglary has not occurred. Trespass could be considered, if Stealing is not preferred).

Scenario 13: An offender climbs a fence into the rear yard of a house and walks into the unenclosed patio. The victim is seen by the offender and climbs back over the fence and runs away.

Offences to record:

• Trespass x 1 (At the time of the offence the patio is not capable of being secured

and consequently a burglary has not occurred).

Scenario 14: A man opens an unlocked door to the house where his ex-partner now resides, walks through the house until he finds her, assaults her and leaves.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1.

• Assault x 1 offence.

Scenario 15: An offender breaks into a house and finds keys for a car which is parked in the garage. The offender opens the garage door and drives away the motor vehicle.

Offences to record:

• Burglary and Commit x 1.

• Stealing (General) x 1 (As keys were not in the motor vehicle at the time it was

stolen, a Stealing offence must be created).

• Stealing of Motor Vehicle x 1. (All stolen motor vehicles must be subject of an

individual offence. It is immaterial that where multiple motor vehicles are stolen that they are owned by the same victim.

Stealing of Motor Vehicle:

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 26 | P a g e

Scenario 1: On leaving the house in the morning, a victim finds that both his cars have been stolen from the driveway during the night.

Offences to record:

• Stealing of Motor Vehicle x 2. (Similar to the recording treatment of burglary, a

motor vehicle is considered to be the victim. All stolen motor vehicles must be subject of an individual offence and it is immaterial whether they are owned by the same victim.)

Scenario 2: An individual reports having their motor vehicle stolen which had a trailer attached containing work tools.

Offences to record:

• Steal Motor Vehicle x 1. (Both registrations to be recorded in ‘Vehicle Panel’)

If the stolen motor vehicle is recovered however the trailer and/or tools remain outstanding an offence of Stealing (General) must be added for the outstanding property.

Scenario 3: A utility is stolen with two dirt bikes (not eligible for registration) in the rear tray.

Offences to record:

• Steal Motor Vehicle x 1.

If the stolen utility is recovered however the dirt bikes remain outstanding an offence of Stealing (General) must be added (All vehicle details to be recorded in Vehicle Panel).

Scenario 4: An offender enters an unlocked motor vehicle and locates the keys which are used to start and drive away the motor vehicle.

Offences to record:

• Steal Motor Vehicle x 1.

A separate Stealing offence is NOT to be created for the keys or other property in the vehicle until such time as the vehicle is recovered and the items are discovered to be missing, or it is determined that the vehicle will not be recovered. A description of the property however MUST be entered into the ‘Property Panel’ of the Incident Report.

Scenario 5: A caravan is taken from the front yard of a residential premises.

Offences to record:

• Stealing (General) x 1. (A caravan is not a motor vehicle. A Stealing (General) is

recorded).

Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts):

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 27 | P a g e

Scenario 1: An offender smashes the driver’s window on a motor vehicle and steals property belonging to the owner of the motor vehicle and another victim.

Offences to record:

• Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) x 1 (2 x victims added to IR).

No damage offence is created as the damage was to facilitate the property theft.

The damage must however be recorded in the Property Panel of the Incident Report.

Scenario 2: An offender breaks into a motor vehicle and steals a wallet belonging to the vehicle owner, along with the rear wheels from the vehicle.

Offences to record:

• Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) x 1 (1 x victim for vehicle and

other property)

Scenario 3: An offender steals the wheels from a hire car, along with some luggage from the boot.

Offences to record:

• Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) x 1 (2 x victims added to IR).

Scenario 4: An offender breaks into the cab of a utility and steals cash and CDS and also steals tools from the rear tray and syphons fuel from the vehicle tank. All property is owned by the same victim.

Offences to record:

• Stealing of Motor Vehicle Contents or Parts x 1 (1 x victim added to IR).

Scenario 5: An offender enters a car sale yard and breaks into ten cars, stealing property from four and nothing from the other six.

Offences to record:

• Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) x 4.

• Attempted Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) x 6.

Scenario 6: An offender steals both registration plates from a motor vehicle.

Offences to record:

• Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) x 1 (1 x victim added to IR).

Scenario 7: An offender steals property from a bus, there are 16 victims Offences to record:

• Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) x 1 (All victims added to IR.

Where the vehicle owner has incurred damage but no stealing, an Attempted Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) could be considered).

Stealing From Retail Premises (Shoplift):

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 28 | P a g e

Scenario 1: Two offenders enter a supermarket and work together in concealing numerous items about their bodies before leaving the store without paying.

Offences to record:

• Stealing from Retail Premises (Shoplift) x 1.

(Single victim and incident. Number of offenders and items stolen is immaterial).

Scenario 2: Two offenders enter a supermarket and work together in concealing numerous items about their bodies before leaving the store without paying. They return to the store ten minutes later and again conceal stolen property about their person before leaving the store without paying.

Offences to record:

• Stealing from Retail Premises (Shoplift) x 2.

(In this case two separate stealing offences have occurred. Again, the number of offenders and number of items stolen is immaterial).

Stealing (General):

Scenario 1: An offender cuts a bicycle lock from a bicycle at a train station and rides off on the bicycle.

Offences to record:

• Stealing (General) x 1.

No damage offence is created as the damage was to facilitate the stealing. The damage must however be recorded in the Property Section of the IR.

Scenario 2: An offender steals a handbag from under a table at a nightclub, which contains the property of the bag owner and the mobile telephone of her friend.

Offences to record:

• Stealing (General) x 1.

Whilst there are two victims, the incident involved a single act of stealing. Both victims should be recorded to the single offence of Stealing (General).

Assault Offences:

Scenario 1: Two persons are having an argument in a house when one lashes out and punches the other. The victim starts moving toward the front door intending to leave and the offender follows him, punching him a further 4 times before the victim is able to leave the house. No injury is caused.

Offences to record:

• Common Assault x 1 (A single continuing assault incident occurred. The

number of blows is irrelevant).

Scenario 2: An elderly man is walking across a park when a group of 8 youths surround him. All the offenders assault him to various degrees by pushing, punching Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 29 | P a g e

and kicking him until he collapses to the ground with injuries consistent with Grievous Bodily Harm.

Offences to record:

• GBH x 1 (whilst individual offenders may ultimately be charged with assault

offences of a lesser degree, the incident is defined by its seriousness as a

GBH).

Damage Offences:

Scenario 1: An offender throws rocks to smash three windows at the front of a building which is occupied by a single business (victim).

Offences to record:

• Damage or Criminal Damage x 1 (Single victim. Number of items damaged is

immaterial).

Scenario 2: An offender smashes three windows on a motor vehicle and steals property.

Offences to record:

• Stealing of Motor Vehicle Contents or Parts x 1.

• Damage or Criminal Damage x 1.

Whilst a Damage offence is not to be created where the damage is caused purely to facilitate a stealing offence, the smashing of multiple windows would be considered an unnecessary and a wilful act and consequently a Damage offence should be created.

Scenario 3: On attending a rental property after tenants have vacated, a real estate agency finds significant damage consistent with the damage having been wilfully committed. This included multiple holes in walls and doors and cabinet doors having being torn from hinges.

Offences to record:

• Criminal Damage x 1 (in circumstances where damage has obviously been

committed through wilful acts, rather than through general wear and tear or by accident, the offence could be created, notwithstanding any potential civil action that the victim may take).

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 30 | P a g e

Annexure E: Outcomes Scenario Guide

When an Offence Outcome Status Code is applied to a recorded offence, the reason for selection of the chosen Outcome must be recorded within Running Sheet of the Incident Report.

Offender Processed

To be applied where offender/s are processed for offences on an IR. Multiple offences should not be created in IMS just to record against multiple offenders.

This Outcome should not be used where other Outcomes are more appropriate (see scenario 1 and 2 below).

Scenario 1: A child under ten years is not criminally responsible, do not use Offender Processed – Statute Barred, when the correct Offence Outcome is Unable to Proceed / Charge.

Scenario 2: A murder – suicide occurs and police attend and investigate. The appropriate Outcome is Unable to Proceed / Charge, not Offender Processed – Deceased (did they die in custody?)

Entered in Error

Entered in Error is to be applied when a data entry error has occurred that has resulted in an offence being added incorrectly.

Scenario 1: On reviewing an Incident Report, a supervisor detects that Burglary and Commit offences have been created against three charged suspects, rather than linking the suspects to the single Burglary and Commit offence.

Outcome: All offenders are to be recorded on the IR, one Burglary and Commit offence should remain and Entered in Error should be applied against the additional offences.

Scenario 2: On reviewing an Incident Report for a stealing from a motor vehicle, a supervisor finds that a Damage offence has been created that relates to the smashing of a front window to gain access to the motor vehicle to steal property.

Outcome: Apply Entered in Error against the Damage offence. If the damage is only caused to facilitate the offence (i.e. gain entry to the motor vehicle), a Damage offence is not to be created. The damage must however be recorded in the Property Section of the Incident Report.

No Criminal Offence

No Criminal Offence is to be applied when investigation concludes that no offence was committed. This includes offences mistakenly reported by the public. This includes incidents that are civil disputes and do not constitute a criminal offence.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 31 | P a g e

Scenario 1: An Incident Report is created with an offence of Stealing of a Motor Vehicle. The victim contacts police to advise it had been mistakenly reported as it was taken by his son who has just returned home with it.

Outcome: Apply No Criminal Offence as it was mistakenly reported.

Scenario 2: A service station provides a completed Fuel Drive-off Reporting Pack to their local police station as PAC advised. Investigating officers view CCTV footage and identify a suspect. They attend the suspect’s home and sight the vehicle of interest. The criminality indicators of non-payment and obscured plates are investigated and officers are satisfied there is no criminal offence.

Outcome: Apply No Criminal Offence. The investigating officers deemed the obscured plate to be unintentional and the suspect advised he had already returned and paid for fuel.

Scenario 3: An Incident Report has been created for a Stealing of Motor Vehicle. A subsequent investigation finds that the complainant and an ex-partner had separated at an earlier time and the ex-partner had possession of the motor vehicle which was jointly purchased during the relationship. The complainant states that the motor vehicle should be returned as the motor vehicle was registered in their name. There is no evidence of criminality in the ex-partner taking possession of the motor vehicle.

Outcome: Apply No Criminal Offence. This is a civil dispute over property ownership.

Scenario 4: A report is received from a victim that he had lent his vehicle to a friend who was a mechanic to undertake some repairs. The friend now refuses to return the car. On speaking with the friend police are advised that the victim requested he undertake some repairs on the vehicle. He has since done so and told the victim he needs to pay the cost of the repairs to have the vehicle returned.

Outcome: Apply No Criminal Offence. This is a civil dispute with there being no evidence of a criminal offence having been committed. The ‘friend’ is claiming a lien.

Scenario 5: A car owner reports that he entered into an agreement to sell his car for $2,000 and let the purchaser take the car following a $1,000 down payment. The car owner states that despite numerous telephone calls, the purchaser refuses to pay the outstanding $1,000 and wants to report the car as being stolen, which has since had ownership transferred into the purchaser’s name.

On investigation, the purchaser states that the agreement was for $1,000 and that the previous owner had since demanded return of the motor vehicle or an additional $1,000 as he had received a subsequent offer of $2,000 for the motor vehicle. The purchaser states he has lawfully purchased and registered the motor vehicle and would neither return it, nor pay any more for it.

Outcome: Apply No Criminal Offence. This is a civil dispute that needs to be handled between the parties.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 32 | P a g e

Scenario 6: A landlord reports that tenants vacating a property stole the garden hose, a number of light globes, some crockery and a microwave oven and damaged tiles in the bathroom and a security screen-door lock. The landlord points out that the rental contract requires the tenants to leave the premises in the same state in which it was rented, inclusive of all light fixtures and garden hoses to be in place. Upon interviewing the former tenants they advise that:

- the light globes had blown at various times and had never been replaced and

a number of items of crockery had been accidently broken over the period of the rental;

- the garden hose was purchased by them after the old one had become

unserviceable and they provide a receipt;

- that the former tenants, on moving out, had given them the microwave oven;

and,

- the damage occurred over time as normal wear and tear.

The tenants refuse to return the garden hose or microwave. A check with the former tenants confirms that the microwave had been left by them at the house and did not belong to the rental property.

Outcome: Apply No Criminal Offence. The entire matter is civil in nature. Light globes, crockery and garden hoses are disposable items and there is reasonable expectation they will fail or be accidently broken. The landlord has no special claim over the microwave oven. The damage could be considered reasonable wear and tear. Breaching clauses of a civil contract does in not a criminal offence. The landlord can recover losses or address contractual breaches through Bond negotiations and civil Court action.

Offence Substituted

Offence Substituted is to be applied when the offence is replaced with an alternate on the basis the previously recorded offence is no longer an accurate reflection of the circumstances.

Scenario 1: A victim reports that their house has been broken into but they do not know what, if anything, has been stolen. An Incident Report is created with an offence of Burglary with Intent. Later the victim contacts police to advise that property has been stolen.

Outcome: Apply Offence Substituted against the Burglary with Intent and create the new offences of Burglary and Commit and Stealing (General).

Scenario 2: A victim reports an assault and an Incident Report is taken for Common Assault. Following medical treatment it is found the victim has suffered bone fractures and consequently it is determined that the correct offence should be Assault Occasioning Bodily Harm.

Outcome: Apply Offence Substituted against the Common Assault and create the new offence of Assault Occasioning Bodily Harm.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 33 | P a g e

Scenario 3: An Incident Report is submitted for Stealing of Motor Vehicle. Sometime later the stolen vehicle is involved in an Evade Police Incident, resulting in a suspect being charged with ‘Stealing of Motor Vehicle and Drive Recklessly’.

Outcome: Apply Offence Substituted against the Stealing of Motor Vehicle offence and create the new offence of Stealing of Motor Vehicle and Drive Recklessly.

Scenario 4: An Incident Report for an offence of Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) has been created an investigation finds that the property has been taken from a trailer, which does not meet the definition of a motor vehicle.

Outcome: Apply Offence Substituted against the Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) and create a Stealing (General) offence.

Scenario 5: A victim reports that their car has been broken into but they do not know what, if anything, has been stolen. An Incident Report is created for Attempted Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts). Later the victim contacts police to advise that property has been stolen.

Outcome: Apply Offence Substituted against the Attempted Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts) and create a new offence of Stealing from Motor Vehicle (Contents or Parts).

Complaint Withdrawn

Complaint Withdrawn is to be applied when an offender cannot be processed due to the victim no longer supporting the investigation and/or processing of the suspect.

Scenario 1: A trailer is hired from a service station with the suspect using his real name but an old address. When the trailer is not returned on the agreed date a service station representative telephones the hirer who states he will return it the following day. When it is not returned, a further attempt to contact the hirer finds the telephone has been disconnected. A representative of the service station attends at the address provided by the hirer and establishes that it is an old rental address for the hirer.

The trailer is subsequently reported stolen and an Incident Report with an offence of Stealing (General) is created. A month later, police locate the trailer during search of a premises and subsequently advised by the service station that they are satisfied with the return of the trailer but do not want to pursue criminal charges. Instead, they will seek civil redress.

Outcome: In the first instance investigators would need to consider whether any known circumstances (e.g. use of false details, deliberate avoidance, history of similar acts, abandonment of property etc.), were suggestive of a criminal offence having been committed in the first instance, or whether a matter was civil. If determined the incident was not criminal, an Outcome of No Criminal Offence should be applied.

If investigators determine the evidence was sufficient to suggest that a criminal offence has occurred, the correct Outcome would be Complaint Withdrawn.

Scenario 2: A landlord reports significant damage within a premises following eviction of the tenants. An inspection by police finds that the damage is beyond what could be Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 34 | P a g e

considered accidental or reasonable wear and tear and included holes in multiple doors and walls and graffiti. The landlord wants the matter reported for insurance purposes but does not want to follow through with criminal charges. He indicates that he will be however exploring civil options with respect to the bond.

Outcome: Complaint Withdrawn to be applied. The damage is evidence that a criminal offence may have occurred in which case an Outcome of No Criminal Offence or Insufficient Evidence should not be applied.

Scenario 3: A Burglary is reported whereby an offender has entered the house through the dog door and stolen a jar containing coins. An investigation finds that the offender was the young nephew of the complainant. Consequently the victim decides she does not want the nephew charged and signs a withdrawal statement.

Outcome: Complaint Withdrawn to be applied. This is not a case of No Criminal Offence. The offence occurred and must be retained in the offence record.

Insufficient Evidence

Insufficient Evidence is to be applied when investigation has determined that a criminal offence occurred and a suspect has been identified, there is not enough evidence to process the suspect.

Scenario 1: An Incident Report is created for an Attempted Burglary whereby a flyscreen had been removed and a window partially open. An investigation identifies a suspect who had been observed climbing over a rear fence and running away.

During interview he admitted to having been in the rear yard but denied attempting to break in and there is no evidence to directly link the offender to the Attempted Burglary.

He is subsequently charged with Trespass.

Outcome: Apply Insufficient Evidence to the Attempted Burglary offence and create a new offence of Trespass against which the suspect will be processed. As the Attempted Burglary has been confirmed to have occurred, it must be retained in the offence record and consequently cannot be subject of Offence Substituted.

Scenario 2: A Burglary is reported whereby highly identifiable property has been stolen. Police later execute a search warrant on a neighbouring premises and recover property stolen from the burglary. The suspect will not admit to the Burglary but tells police he was given the property by someone he would not name. There is no evidence directly linking the suspect to the Burglary and it is decided to charge the suspect with Possession of Unlawfully Obtained Property.

Outcome: Apply Insufficient Evidence to the Burglary offence and create a new offence of Possession of Unlawfully Obtained Property which would be resulted as Offender Processed.

Scenario 3: A police station receives a completed Fuel Drive-off Reporting Pack.

Investigations discover the vehicle has been involved in previous similar incidents and overt acts to avoid payment are observed on CCTV that suggest there was no intention to pay. Police speak with the vehicle owner who states he does not know who was driving the vehicle and the vehicle owner does not resemble the offender in the CCTV footage. There are no further avenues of investigation.

Crime Recording User Guide Version 2.1 35 | P a g e

Outcome: On the basis that investigations appear to substantially support that a Stealing offence did occur, apply an Outcome of ‘Insufficient Evidence’ to the Stealing (General) offence which will retain it in the offence record.

Unable to Proceed / Charge

Scenario 1: Fingerprints are recovered from an abandoned stolen motor vehicle which are identified as being those of a known local offender. Prior to police being able to locate the suspect, he is killed in a motor vehicle crash.

Outcome: Apply Unable to Proceed / Charge.

Scenario 2: A Burglary occurs and inquiries identify a suspect who is 9 years of age.

Consequently the suspect is deemed as being not criminally responsible.

Outcome: Apply Unable to Proceed / Charge.

Scenario 3: During an investigation of a recorded offence of Conduct likely to incite racial animosity or racist harassment, it has become necessary to consult with the ODPP to obtain permission to charge and prosecute. Following consideration of the matter, the ODPP direct that no charges are to be laid for reasons other than insufficient evidence.

Outcome: Apply Unable to Proceed / Charge. The offence will remain and be counted in the offence record.
